claims.claim_text: CD4+ and CD8+ T cells are significant in the healing response and bone regeneration post-implantation, promoting bone healing and bone-implant contact @avery2023.

CD4+ T cells exhibit a more significant immunomodulatory role in neutrophil clearance, macrophage polarization, MSC recruitment, and promoting bone-implant contact at the peri-implant site @avery2024.
The absence or deficiency of alphabeta T cells compromises new bone formation and attenuates MSC recruitment and proliferation in vivo and in vitro @avery25.
T cell activation contributes significantly to the inflammatory response to implanted biomaterials, influencing macrophage polarization and MSC recruitment/proliferation @avery23.
Deficiency of CD4+ or CD8+ T cells skews the MSC recruitment and innate immune cell response to modified Ti implants in vivo, and alters the peri-implant cytokine profile @avery24.
Deficiency of alphabeta T cells promotes macrophage polarization towards a pro-inflammatory phenotype and attenuates MSC recruitment and proliferation @avery25.
The Th2 and Treg subsets are upregulated in response to macrophages on rough-hydrophilic Ti implants, correlating with higher anti-inflammatory macrophages and an anti-inflammatory effect @avery23.
Adoptive transfer of immunomodulatory macrophages can reduce the pro-inflammatory microenvironment and increase bone formation on titanium implants @avery23.
CD4+ and CD8+ T cells reduce inflammation and promote bone healing in response to titanium implants @avery2023.
CD4+ or CD8+ T cells enhance bone formation and bone implant contact in response to modified Ti implants @avery2023.
CD4+ T cells play a significant immunomodulatory role in neutrophil clearance, macrophage polarization, MSC recruitment, and promoting bone-implant contact at the peri-implant site @avery2024.

T cell activation is significant during the inflammatory response to implanted biomaterials, contributing to macrophage polarization and MSC recruitment and proliferation, and the absence of alphabeta T cells compromises new bone formation at the implantation site @avery2025.
Deficiency of CD4+ or CD8+ T cells attenuates inflammatory cytokine production in response to modified Ti implants @avery2025.

Mice deficient in CD4+ or CD8+ T cells exhibit altered populations of neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, anti-inflammatory macrophages, and MSCs in response to modified Ti implants @avery2025.
CD4+ T cell subsets can be influenced by macrophage response to surface roughness and wettability of Ti implants @avery2025.

Th2 and Treg subsets are upregulated in response to macrophages on rough-hydrophilic Ti implants, correlating with higher anti-inflammatory macrophages and exhibiting an anti-inflammatory effect @avery2025.

The absence of alphabeta T cells compromises new bone formation and attenuates MSC recruitment and proliferation in vitro and in vivo, and promotes macrophage polarization towards a pro-inflammatory phenotype @avery2025.
Deficiency of CD4+ or CD8+ T cells skews MSC recruitment and innate immune cell response to modified Ti implants in vivo @avery2025.
CD4+ and CD8+ T cell subsets impact the inflammatory response to titanium (Ti) biomaterials @avery2025.
T cell subsets are involved in modulating immune cell crosstalk and mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) recruitment following biomaterial implantation @avery2025.
CD4+ T cells play a more significant immunomodulatory role in neutrophil clearance, macrophage polarization, MSC recruitment, and promoting bone-implant contact at the peri-implant site @avery2024.
Deficiency of alphabeta T cells promotes macrophage polarization towards a pro-inflammatory phenotype and attenuates MSC recruitment and proliferation in vitro and in vivo @avery2025.

Deficiency of alphabeta T cells results in higher pro-inflammatory macrophage phenotype, lower anti-inflammatory macrophages, and significantly reduced recruitment and proliferation of MSCs in response to modified Ti @avery2025.
CD4+ T cells are a significant source of anti-inflammatory cytokines like IL-4, which is vital for suppressing pro-inflammatory macrophages and activating anti-inflammatory macrophages @avery2025.
Macrophage polarization and T cell subsets are critical for the healing response and bone regeneration post-implantation, influencing tissue integration @avery2024.
Macrophages, independently of their phenotype, are critical for the healing process and osseointegration of the Ti implant @avery2025.

Short-term ablation of macrophages can reduce T cell recruitment, increase neutrophils, and increase pro-inflammatory cytokines in the peri-implant tissue, leading to decreased immunomodulatory IL-10 compared to macrophage-competent mice @avery2025.

Alphabeta T cell subsets play an important role in the inflammatory response to Ti implantation by modulating innate immune cells and facilitating MSC recruitment and proliferation for tissue integration and bone formation @avery2025.
CD4+ and CD8+ T cells enhance bone formation and bone implant contact in response to modified Ti implants @avery2023.
Deficiency of CD4+ or CD8+ T cells promotes macrophage polarization towards a pro-inflammatory phenotype and attenuates MSC recruitment and proliferation in vitro and in vivo @avery2025.
Deficiency of CD4+ or CD8+ T cells alters the peri-implant cytokine profile in response to modified Ti in vivo @avery2025.
Inflammatory cytokine production specific to T cells is attenuated in mice deficient in CD4+ or CD8+ T cells in response to modified Ti implants @avery2025.
The role of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells in the foreign body response is significant, impacting macrophage polarization, MSC recruitment, and new bone formation @avery2023, @avery2024, @avery2025.
